title: The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe
author: C.S. Lewis

content.text: Once upon a time, in the heart of England, there stood a grand old house belonging to an old professor.
It was a place of many rooms, long corridors, and secret nooks—just the sort of house where adventures might begin.
Into this house came four children: Peter, Susan, Edmund, and Lucy Pevensie. They had been sent away from London during the war, for the city was no longer safe, and the
professor’s home was far in the countryside, where the air was clean and the nights were quiet. The children explored the house eagerly, for it was vast and full of wonders.
One rainy afternoon, while playing hide-and-seek, Lucy—the youngest—slipped into a room that was almost empty, save for a large wooden wardrobe standing against
the wall. She opened the door and stepped inside, expecting to find coats and furs. Instead, her fingers brushed against something cold and prickly—pine needles!
And then, to her astonishment, she found herself standing not in a wardrobe at all, but in the middle of a snowy forest. Lucy gasped.
The air was crisp, and the trees stretched endlessly before her, their branches heavy with snow. She took a few steps forward and saw a lamppost glowing faintly in the distance.
How strange! She had never seen a lamppost in the middle of a forest before. Curious, she walked toward it, and as she did, she heard the crunch of snow underfoot and the distant call
of a bird. Then, from behind a tree, stepped a most unusual creature—a faun! He was half man, half goat, with curly brown hair, a short beard, and little horns upon his forehead.
He carried an umbrella and several brown-paper parcels, and he looked just as surprised to see Lucy as she was to see him. “Goodness gracious!” exclaimed the faun.
“Are you a Daughter of Eve?” Lucy, though startled, answered politely, “I’m Lucy Pevensie. Who are you?” “I am Mr. Tumnus,” said the faun with a bow.
“And this, my dear, is the land of Narnia—a place where it is always winter but never Christmas.” Lucy’s eyes widened. “Winter but never Christmas? That sounds dreadful!” Mr.
Tumnus sighed. “It is the work of the White Witch. She has cast a spell over Narnia so that it is always cold, always dark. But come, let us not speak of such things.
Would you like to come to my home for tea?” Lucy, though a little nervous, agreed. Mr. Tumnus led her to a cozy little cave, where a fire crackled in the hearth and a kettle hummed.
They sat together, sipping tea and eating toast, while Mr. Tumnus played sweet tunes on his flute. But as the evening wore on, Lucy noticed that her host seemed troubled. “Mr.
Tumnus,” she said at last, “is something wrong?” The faun looked at her with tears in his eyes. “My dear child, I am under orders from the White Witch.
If ever I meet a Son of Adam or a Daughter of Eve in the woods, I am to capture them and take them to her. And I… I was going to do just that. But now that I’ve met you, I cannot.”
Lucy’s heart pounded. “Oh, please don’t! I must go home at once!” Mr. Tumnus nodded sadly. “I will take you back to the lamppost.
But you must promise never to tell anyone about me—or about Narnia.” Lucy promised, and Mr. Tumnus led her back through the snowy woods to the lamppost.
With one last glance, she stepped back into the wardrobe and found herself in the empty room once more. Lucy rushed out to find her siblings. “Peter! Susan! Edmund! I’ve been to Narnia!”
she cried. But they only laughed. “Don’t be silly, Lucy,” said Edmund. “You’ve been gone for barely a minute.” Lucy insisted, but the others did not believe her.
The next day, however, while playing hide-and-seek again, Edmund followed Lucy into the wardrobe. To his amazement, he too found himself in Narnia. But instead of meeting Mr.
Tumnus, he encountered a sleigh pulled by reindeer, and upon it sat a woman with a cold, beautiful face—the White Witch. “Who are you?” she demanded.
“I’m Edmund Pevensie,” he said, bowing awkwardly. The Witch smiled—a smile that sent a chill down Edmund’s spine. “You are a Son of Adam,” she said. “Come, sit with me.
I have a treat for you—Turkish Delight, the finest in all the world.” Edmund, who had a terrible sweet tooth, eagerly accepted.
The Witch gave him a box of enchanted Turkish Delight, each piece more delicious than the last. But as he ate, he felt a strange pull—a desire to do whatever the Witch asked.
“Bring your brother and sisters to my castle,” she said. “I will make you a prince, and one day, a king.” Edmund, dazzled by the promise, agreed.
Little did he know that the Witch’s Turkish Delight was enchanted, and that she had no intention of keeping her word.
Meanwhile, Lucy had returned to Narnia, and this time, Peter and Susan followed. They met Mr. Tumnus, who told them that the White Witch had taken him prisoner for helping Lucy.
The children were horrified and vowed to rescue him. As they journeyed deeper into Narnia, they met a talking beaver who told them of Aslan, the great lion who was the true
ruler of Narnia. “Aslan is on the move,” said Mr. Beaver. “The Witch’s power is weakening. But we must hurry—Edmund has gone to her!”
The children set off at once, following the beavers to the Stone Table, where Aslan was said to be gathering his forces.
Along the way, they encountered wonders—talking animals, friendly giants, and even Father Christmas himself, who gave them gifts to aid in their quest.
But Edmund, under the Witch’s spell, had already reached her castle. She was furious when he told her that his siblings were in Narnia. “You have failed me!” she hissed.
“But no matter—I will find them myself.” With a wave of her wand, she turned a group of merry forest creatures into stone.
Then, with Edmund in tow, she set off in her sleigh to find the others. Back at the Stone Table, Aslan welcomed Peter, Susan, and Lucy.
He was a magnificent lion, golden and noble, and though his voice was deep and gentle, there was a power in it that made the children feel both afraid and safe.
“Edmund has gone to the Witch,” Aslan said. “But we must save him.” The next day, a battle raged between Aslan’s forces and the Witch’s army.
Peter fought bravely, but the Witch was powerful, and it seemed she might win.
Then, just as all hope seemed lost, Aslan arrived with reinforcements—giants, centaurs, and even trees that had come to life!
The Witch’s army was defeated, and Edmund was rescued. But the Witch had not given up. She demanded Edmund’s life, for the Deep Magic of Narnia said that any traitor belonged
to her. Aslan spoke with her in private, and though the children did not hear what was said, they saw the Witch’s face twist with triumph. That night, Susan and Lucy could not sleep.
They crept out of their tent and saw Aslan walking sadly toward the Stone Table. The Witch and her followers were there, and they bound Aslan and shaved his mane.
The Witch raised her knife—and with a terrible cry, she struck him down. The girls wept, but as the first light of dawn touched the Stone Table, something miraculous happened.
The ropes fell away, and Aslan stood before them—alive and more glorious than ever! “How is this possible?” whispered Lucy.
Aslan explained that there was a Deeper Magic, older than the Witch’s, that said if an innocent victim gave his life for a traitor, death itself would be undone.
With Aslan’s return, the spell over Narnia was broken. The snow melted, flowers bloomed, and the creatures turned to stone by the Witch were restored.
The children, now crowned as kings and queens, ruled Narnia for many years, bringing peace and joy to the land.
But one day, while hunting a white stag, they stumbled upon the lamppost once more.
Without thinking, they followed it—and found themselves back in the wardrobe, and then in the empty room, as if no time had passed at all.
“Well,” said Peter, brushing himself off, “that was the most peculiar adventure.” Lucy smiled. “But it was real, wasn’t it?”
And though they never spoke of it again, they all knew the truth—Narnia would always be waiting, just a wardrobe away.
